name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَرْيَمَ إِذِ ٱنتَبَذَتْ مِنْ أَهْلِهَا مَكَانًا شَرْقِيًّا (١٦)
```

```english
And mention in the Book Mary, when she withdrew from her family to an easterly place. (16)
```

```arabic
فَٱتَّخَذَتْ مِن دُونِهِمْ حِجَابًا فَأَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهَا رُوحَنَا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَهَا بَشَرًا سَوِيًّا (١٧)
```

```english
Thus did she seclude herself from them, whereupon We sent to her Our Spirit and he became incarnate for her as a well-proportioned human. (17)
```

```arabic
قَالَتْ إِنِّىٓ أَعُوذُ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مِنكَ إِن كُنتَ تَقِيًّا (١٨)
```

```english
She said, ‘I seek the protection of the All-beneficent from you, should you be Godwary!’ (18)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلَـٰمًا زَكِيًّا (١٩)
```

```english
He said, ‘I am only a messenger of your Lord that I may give you a pure son.’ (19)
```

```arabic
قَالَتْ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌ وَلَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا (٢٠)
```

```english
She said, ‘How shall I have a child seeing that no human being has ever touched me, nor have I been unchaste?’ (20)
```

```arabic
قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ ۖ وَلِنَجْعَلَهُۥٓ ءَايَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَرَحْمَةً مِّنَّا ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرًا مَّقْضِيًّا (٢١)
```

```english
He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is simple for Me.’’ And so that We may make him a sign for mankind and a mercy from Us, and it is a matter [already] decided.’ (21)
```

```arabic
۞ فَحَمَلَتْهُ فَٱنتَبَذَتْ بِهِۦ مَكَانًا قَصِيًّا (٢٢)
```

```english
Thus she conceived him, then withdrew with him to a distant place. (22)
```

```arabic
فَأَجَآءَهَا ٱلْمَخَاضُ إِلَىٰ جِذْعِ ٱلنَّخْلَةِ قَالَتْ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى مِتُّ قَبْلَ هَـٰذَا وَكُنتُ نَسْيًا مَّنسِيًّا (٢٣)
```

```english
The birth pangs brought her to the trunk of a date palm. She said, ‘I wish I had died before this and become a forgotten thing, beyond recall.’ (23)
```

```arabic
فَنَادَىٰهَا مِن تَحْتِهَآ أَلَّا تَحْزَنِى قَدْ جَعَلَ رَبُّكِ تَحْتَكِ سَرِيًّا (٢٤)
```

```english
Thereupon he called her from below her [saying,] ‘Do not grieve! Your Lord has made a spring to flow at your feet. (24)
```

```arabic
وَهُزِّىٓ إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ ٱلنَّخْلَةِ تُسَـٰقِطْ عَلَيْكِ رُطَبًا جَنِيًّا (٢٥)
```

```english
Shake the trunk of the palm tree, freshly picked dates will drop upon you. (25)
```

```arabic
فَكُلِى وَٱشْرَبِى وَقَرِّى عَيْنًا ۖ فَإِمَّا تَرَيِنَّ مِنَ ٱلْبَشَرِ أَحَدًا فَقُولِىٓ إِنِّى نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ صَوْمًا فَلَنْ أُكَلِّمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ إِنسِيًّا (٢٦)
```

```english
Eat, drink, and be comforted. Then if you see any human, say, ‘‘Indeed I have vowed a fast to the All-beneficent, so I will not speak to any human today.’’ ’ (26)
```

```arabic
فَأَتَتْ بِهِۦ قَوْمَهَا تَحْمِلُهُۥ ۖ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ لَقَدْ جِئْتِ شَيْـًٔا فَرِيًّا (٢٧)
```

```english
Then carrying him she brought him to her people. They said, ‘O Mary, you have certainly come up with an odd thing! (27)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأُخْتَ هَـٰرُونَ مَا كَانَ أَبُوكِ ٱمْرَأَ سَوْءٍ وَمَا كَانَتْ أُمُّكِ بَغِيًّا (٢٨)
```

```english
O sister of Aaron[’s lineage]! Your father was not an evil man, nor was your mother unchaste.’ (28)
```

```arabic
فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا (٢٩)
```

```english
Thereat she pointed to him. They said, ‘How can we speak to one who is yet a baby in the cradle?’ (29)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّا (٣٠)
```

```english
He said, ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet. (30)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَـٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا (٣١)
```

```english
He has made me blessed, wherever I may be, and He has enjoined me to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat as long as I live, (31)
```

```arabic
وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَتِى وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِى جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا (٣٢)
```

```english
and to be good to my mother, and He has not made me self-willed and wretched. (32)
```

```arabic
وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا (٣٣)
```

```english
Peace to me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive.’ (33)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۚ قَوْلَ ٱلْحَقِّ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
That is Jesus, son of Mary, a Word of the Real concerning whom they are in doubt. (34)
```

```arabic
مَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ أَن يَتَّخِذَ مِن وَلَدٍ ۖ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٣٥)
```

```english
It is not for Allah to take a son. Immaculate is He! When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (35)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٣٦)
```

```english
[And Jesus said,] ‘Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord. So worship Him. This is a straight path.’ (36)
```

```arabic
فَٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَيْنِهِمْ ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن مَّشْهَدِ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٣٧)
```

```english
But the factions differed among themselves. So woe to the faithless at the scene of a tremendous day. (37)
```

```arabic
أَسْمِعْ بِهِمْ وَأَبْصِرْ يَوْمَ يَأْتُونَنَا ۖ لَـٰكِنِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٨)
```

```english
How well they will hear and how well they will see on the day when they come to Us! But today the wrongdoers are in manifest error. (38)
```

```arabic
وَأَنذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْحَسْرَةِ إِذْ قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ وَهُمْ فِى غَفْلَةٍ وَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
Warn them of the Day of Regret, when the matter will be decided, while they are [yet] heedless and do not have faith. (39)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَنْ عَلَيْهَا وَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ (٤٠)
```

```english
Indeed We shall inherit the earth and whoever there is on it, and to Us they shall be brought back. (40)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a transition from the story of Yahya to the story of ‘Isa (peace be upon him). Between the two stories, there is a complete resemblance: the birth of both was through a breach of natural norms; ‘Isa was granted maturity and prophethood while still a child, just like Yahya; he declared that he was dutiful to his mother and was not a tyrannical wretch; and peace was upon him the day he was born, the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive, just as Allah informed regarding Yahya (peace be upon him). This is in addition to other points of resemblance, and Yahya indeed testified to ‘Isa and believed in him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مَرْيَمَ إِذِ انْتَبَذَتْ مِنْ أَهْلِها مَكاناً شَرْقِيًّا** — **And mention Mary in the Book, when she withdrew from her family to an easterly place** (19:16). What is meant by "the Book" (الكتاب) is either the Quran or the Surah itself, for it is a part of the Book, and a part of the Book is also called a book. Both possibilities lead to the same conclusion, so there is little utility in the insistence of some to prioritize or specify the second possibility.

The term **"discarding" (النبذ)** — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is to cast away something insignificant that is not cared for. It is said he "discarded it" (نبذه) when he threw it away, regarding it as contemptible and paying it no heed. **"Withdrawing" (الانتباذ)** means secluding oneself from people and being alone.

Maryam is the daughter of ‘Imran, the mother of the Messiah (peace be upon him). What is meant by "Maryam" is the news of Maryam, and His saying "when" (إِذِ) is an adverbial qualifier for it. His saying "she withdrew" (انْتَبَذَتْ) until the end of the story is the detail of the event contained within that news. The meaning is: Mention, O Muhammad, in this Book the news of Maryam when she secluded herself from her family in an easterly place, which seems to have been the eastern side of the Mosque.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاتَّخَذَتْ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ حِجاباً فَأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا** — **Thus she secluded herself from them with a curtain. Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he appeared to her as a harmonious human** (19:17). The **"curtain" (الحجاب)** is that which veils a thing and conceals it from others. It seems she took the curtain to separate herself from her family so she could cut herself off from them and devote herself to worship (الاعتكاف للعبادة), as indicated by His saying: **كُلَّما دَخَلَ عَلَيْها زَكَرِيَّا الْمِحْرابَ وَجَدَ عِنْدَها رِزْقاً** — **Whenever Zechariah visited her in the sanctuary, he would find provisions with her** (3:37). The discussion regarding the commentary (تفسير) of this verse has already passed.

It has been said: She used to reside in the Mosque, and when she menstruated, she would leave and stay in the house of Zechariah until she became pure, then she would return to the Mosque. While she was in an elevated chamber in a corner of the house, having placed a curtain between herself and her family to wash herself, Gabriel entered upon her in the form of a beardless youth of harmonious build, and she sought refuge in Allah from him.

However, there is no evidence for this detail from the wording of the text. You have already seen that the verse in Surah Al ‘Imran is not devoid of support for the previous meaning.

Regarding His saying: **فَأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا** — **Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he appeared to her as a harmonious human**, the apparent context is that the subject of "appeared" (فَتَمَثَّلَ) is a pronoun referring back to the Spirit. Thus, the Spirit sent to her is the one who appeared to her as a harmonious human. The meaning of his "appearing" (تمثله) to her as a human is his becoming visible to her, appearing to her senses in the form of a human, while in himself he is a spirit and not a human.

Since he was not a human and not from the Jinn, he was an angel (ملك) — meaning the third type of creation that Allah, the Glorified, described in His Book and named an angel. He, the Glorified, mentioned the Angel of Revelation in His speech and named him Gabriel in His saying: **مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ** — **Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart** (2:97). He named him a spirit in His saying: **قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** — **Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord** (16:102), and His saying: **نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ** — **The Trustworthy Spirit brought it down to your heart** (26:193-194). He also named him a messenger in His saying: **إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كريمٍ** — **it is indeed the speech of a noble messenger** (69:40). All of this supports that the Spirit Allah sent to her was indeed Gabriel.

As for His saying: **إِذْ قالَتِ الْمَلائِكَةُ يا مَرْيَمُ إِنَّ اللهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ** — **When the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah gives you the good news of a Word from Him whose name is Messiah, Jesus son of Mary...** — until He said — **قالَتْ رَبِّ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِي بَشَرٌ قالَ كَذلِكِ اللهُ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ إِذا قَضى أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** — **She said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a child seeing that no human has ever touched me?’ He said, ‘So it is: Allah creates whatever He wishes. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘‘Be!’’ and it is’** (3:45-47).

Applying this to the verses we are currently discussing leaves no doubt that the speech of the angels to Maryam and their dialogue with her mentioned there is the same as the speech of the Spirit to her mentioned here. Attributing the speech of Gabriel to "the angels" (الملائكة) is like attributing the speech of one individual of a group to the whole group because they share in nature, tradition, or custom. There is much of this in the Quran, such as His saying, the Exalted: **يَقُولُونَ لَئِنْ رَجَعْنا إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَّ** — **They say, ‘If we return to the city, the ones at the peak of might will surely expel from it the ones at the peak of abasement’** (63:8), while the speaker was only one person. And His saying: **وَإِذْ قالُوا اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ فَأَمْطِرْ عَلَيْنا حِجارَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ** — **And when they said, ‘O Allah, if this is indeed the truth from You, then rain down on us stones from the sky’** (8:32), while the speaker was one.

The annexation of the Spirit to Himself, the Exalted, is for honor (التشريف) while suggesting magnification (التعظيم). A discussion on the meaning of the Spirit (الروح) has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ** — **They question you concerning the Spirit** (17:85).

Among the poor interpretations (التفسير الردي) is the claim of some that the "Spirit" in the verse refers to ‘Isa (peace be upon him) and the pronoun in "appeared" (تمثل) refers to Gabriel. This is as you see [evidently weak].

Among the poor recitations (القراءة الردية) is the recitation of some as "Ruhanna" (روحنا) with a shadda on the *nun*, suggesting that *Ruhanna* is the name of the angel sent to Maryam, and that he is other than Gabriel, the Trustworthy Spirit. This is also as you see [evidently weak].

## (A Discussion on the Meaning of Apparition)

The term "apparition" (التمثل) — *the manifestation of a reality in a form other than its own essence to the perceiver* — is frequently mentioned in the narrations (الروايات). As for the Book, it is only mentioned in the story of Maryam in her Surah, where the Exalted says: **فَأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا** — **Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he appeared to her as a harmonious human** (19:17). The subsequent verses in which Gabriel identifies himself to Maryam are the best evidence that during his apparition to her in the form of a human, he remained in his angelic state and did not actually become a human. Rather, he appeared in the form of a human while not being a human but an angel; it was only Maryam who saw him in the form of a human.

Thus, the meaning of his "appearing" (تمثله) to her as such is his manifestation to her in the form of a human, without that form being his actual self. This means that within the realm of her perception (ظرف إدراكها), he was in the form of a human, while outside of her perception, he was otherwise.

This is what aligns with the linguistic meaning of *tamaththul* (التمثل), for the meaning of one thing appearing to another in such-and-all form is its being conceptualized by the perceiver in that form while it remains what it is, not the transformation of the thing into another thing. Thus, the apparition of the angel as a human is his manifestation to the observer in the form of a human, not the angel becoming a human. If the apparition occurred in the thing itself and outside the realm of perception, it would be a case of a thing becoming another thing and transforming (الانقلاب) into it, not a case of appearing to someone as such.

The matter of this apparition has been challenged by several points mentioned in *al-Tafsir al-Kabir* and elsewhere:

First: Gabriel is a being of immense physical size according to the reports (الأخبار). When did he become the size of a human body? If his parts exceeding the size of a human body fell away, it would necessitate that Gabriel no longer exists. If they did not fall away, it would necessitate their interpenetration (التداخل), which is impossible.

Second: If apparition were possible, certainty would be lost, and it would be impossible to be sure that this person seen now is the same Zayd seen yesterday, due to the possibility of apparition.

Third: If apparition in the form of a human were possible, then apparition in the form of others, such as mosquitoes, insects, and so on, would also be possible. It is known that any doctrine leading to this is false.

Fourth: If this were possible, trust in mass-transmitted reports (الخبر المتواتر) would be lost, such as the report of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fighting on the day of Badr, due to the possibility that the one fighting was an apparition.

The first was answered by saying: It is not impossible for Gabriel to have a few essential parts and many additional parts, and he is able, through those parts, to appear as a human. This is based on the view that he is a body (جسم). As for the view that he is a spiritual being (روحاني), there is no improbability in him donning a large structure at one time and a small structure at another.

You can see that the first part of this answer, like the objection itself, is based on the idea that apparition is a change in the appearing entity itself, the nullification of its first form, and its transition to another form. It has already been stated that apparition is its manifestation in a certain form while it remains in itself otherwise.

The verse, in its context, is clear that Gabriel did not cease to be an angel through apparition, nor did he become a human in himself. He only appeared to her in the form of a human; thus, he was so within the realm of her perception, not in himself or outside the realm of her perception. Similar to this is the descent of the noble angels in the story of the good news of Ishaq and their apparition to Ibrahim and Lut (peace be upon them) in the form of humans. Another example is the appearance of Iblis in the form of Suraqah ibn Malik on the day of Badr, as the Exalted alluded to in Surah al-Anfal (8:48), while Suraqah was in Mecca that day.

In the narrations, there is much of this, such as the apparition of Iblis on the Day of the Assembly (يوم الندوة) to the polytheists in the form of an old man; his apparition on the Day of ‘Aqabah in the form of Munabbih ibn al-Hajjaj; his apparition to Yahya (peace be upon him) in a strange form; and the apparition of the world (الدنيا) to ‘Ali (peace be upon him) in the form of a beautiful, seductive woman, as in the narration. Also, what has been reported regarding the apparition of wealth, children, and deeds to a person at the time of death, and the apparition of deeds to a person in the grave and on the Day of Resurrection. Of this category are dream apparitions, such as the apparition of an enemy in the form of a dog, snake, or scorpion; the apparition of a spouse in the form of a shoe; the apparition of high status in the form of a horse; and pride in the form of a crown, and so on.

The appearing entity in most of these cases — as you see — consists of meanings (المعاني) that have no form or shape in themselves. In them, no change from one form to another or from one shape to another occurs, which is the basis upon which the objection and the answer were built.

The second was answered by saying: This objection applies equally to all, for whoever acknowledges the All-Powerful Creator must accept this, as it is possible for the Exalted to create someone exactly like Zayd, for example. Thus, trust would be lost and certainty would be impossible in the same way mentioned in the objection. Likewise, whoever does not acknowledge the Creator and attributes events to natural causes or celestial configurations must allow for the possibility that causes could lead to the occurrence of someone like Zayd, so the objection returns.

Perhaps because such events are rare (نادر), they do not necessitate a flaw in conventional knowledge (العلوم العادية) based on sensation. Thus, it does not necessitate doubting that the Zayd we observe now is the same Zayd we observed yesterday.

You are well aware that this answer does not uproot the source of the objection. For conceding the divergence between the sensation (الحس) and the sensed object (المحسوس) — such as seeing someone other than Zayd in the form of Zayd — even if rare, nullifies sensory knowledge. It would only remain to claim that it is called "knowledge" because the rarity of deviation and error causes a person to be heedless of turning toward doubt or the possibility of divergence between the sensation and the sensed object.

Furthermore, if divergence were possible and its occurrence were potential in every single case, we would have no way of knowing it to be rare. From where would one know that such a thing is rare in existence?

The truth is that both the objection and the answer are fundamentally flawed:

As for the objection, it is based on the premise that what the sense attains is the very external sensed object in its externality, rather than the image taken from it. From this follows the heedlessness of the meaning of sensory judgments being self-evident (بديهية), and the heedlessness of the fact that projecting the sensory judgment onto the external sensed object is done through thought and reflection (الفكر والنظر), not by the sense itself.

What the sense attains from the external entity is some of its qualities and forms that resemble it in general, not the external thing itself. Then, experience and reflection reveal its state in itself. The proof for this is the various types of divergence between the sensation and the external sensed object, which are called "sensory illusions" (أغلاط الحس), such as seeing the large as small, the high as low, the straight as bent, and the moving as stationary, and vice versa, depending on the different perspectives. The same applies to the judgments of other senses. For example, we see a human individual from a long distance as small as possible, yet we judge through repeated sensation and experience that he is a human similar to us in physical size. We observe the sun to be the size of a platter revolving around the earth, then mathematical proofs lead us to the fact that it is many times larger than the earth and that it is the earth that revolves around the sun.

Thus, it becomes clear that what is truly sensed by us is the image (الصورة) that is within the realm of our sensation, not the external matter in its externality. Furthermore, we do not doubt that what we have sensed, which is in our sensation, has indeed been sensed by us — and this is the meaning of the self-evidence of sensation (بداهة الحس). As for the sensed object, which is that which is external to us and our sensation, the judgment we make upon it arises only from our thought and reflection. This is what we said: that which we believe regarding the state of the external thing is a judgment arising from thought and reflection, not from the sense itself. It has been explained in the sciences discussing the sense and the sensed object that the sensory apparatuses have types of manipulation (تصرف) over the sensed object.

Furthermore, it is necessary for us to believe that outside of our perception, there is a cause (سبب) by which our souls are affected, thus perceiving what they perceive. This cause is sometimes external, such as bodies whose qualities and shapes are linked to our souls through the senses, so we perceive images of them through sensation, then we obtain through experience or thought something of their reality in themselves. Sometimes it is internal, such as intense fear suddenly occurring to a person, which portrays to him terrifying and formidable images according to the painful delusions and thoughts he possesses.

In all these states, a person may be correct in his identification of the state of the external sensed object — which is most common — and he may be mistaken, like one who sees a mirage and estimates it to be water, or shadows and thinks they are persons.

It has become clear from all the preceding that the divergence between the sensation and the external sensed object in itself — while being inevitable in general — does not necessitate the loss of trust or the nullification of reliance on sensation. For the matter in that regard revolves around what a person has obtained from experience, reflection, or otherwise, and the most truthful of them is what experience has verified.

As for the reason for the flaw in the answer, it is its basis on conceding what was conceded in the objection: that the sense attains the very external sensed object itself, and that knowledge of the sensed object in itself is based on the sense itself, with deviation being rare.

The third objection was answered by saying that the basic possibility of an angel appearing in the forms of other animals besides humans is established in principle, and its falsity is only known through traditional proofs (دلائل السمع).

However, there is no traditional evidence of any significance. Rather, the response to the core of the objection is that if by "possibility" (الإمكان) is meant the possibility that stands in contrast to necessity and impossibility, then it is clear that the apparition of an angel in the form of a human does not necessitate the possibility of his apparition in the form of other animals. If by it is meant possibility in the sense of intellectual probability (الاحتمال العقلي), then there is no harm in the probability until a proof is established to negate or affirm it.

The fourth objection was answered with something similar to the answer for the third: that the probability of deviation exists in mass-transmitted reports, but traditional proofs repel it. However, a similar probability exists in the traditional proof itself, for the path to it is the sense of hearing. The correct answer to this objection is the one we provided as an answer to the second objection. And Allah knows best.

It has become clear from what we have presented that apparition (التمثل) is the manifestation of a thing to a human in a form that the human is familiar with and which suits the purpose for which the manifestation occurs. Examples include the apparition of Gabriel to Maryam in the form of a harmonious human because the human understanding of a message is that a human carries the message, then comes to the recipient and delivers what he carries of the message through speech and address; and the apparition of the world to ‘Ali (peace be upon him) in the form of a beautiful woman to entice him, because a woman of exquisite beauty is, in the realm of desires and psychological pleasures, the strongest means used to captivate the heart and overcome the intellect; and other such examples that have been reported.

If you say: This necessitates subscribing to sophistry (السفسطة), for a perception that does not have a reality behind it that corresponds to it in all aspects is nothing but a mirage-like delusion and a false imagination, and its return is to sophistry.

I say: There is a difference between there being a reality that appears to the perceiver through the forms he is familiar with and which his perceptual tools can accommodate, and there being nothing there but a perceptual image with nothing behind it. Sophistry is the latter, not the former. Seeking more than that in the realm of acquired knowledge (العلم الحصولي) is a desire for that which cannot be attained. The full discussion of this is deferred to its proper place. And Allah is the Guide.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَتْ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِالرَّحْمنِ مِنْكَ إِنْ كُنْتَ تَقِيًّا** — **She said, ‘I seek the protection of the All-beneficent from you, should you be God-conscious’** (19:18). She hastened to speak to him because his presence startled her, as she thought he was a human who had intruded upon her for some harmful purpose. She sought refuge in the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) to draw upon the general divine mercy, which is the ultimate hope of those who devote themselves to Him among the people of obedience.

Her condition in saying "should you be God-conscious" (إِنْ كُنْتَ تَقِيًّا) is like a condition based on an attribute that the addressee claims for himself or which is established in him, in order to emphasize the absolute nature of the conditional judgment and the reliance of the judgment upon that attribute. God-consciousness (التقوى) — *the state of being mindful of Allah's boundaries* — is a beautiful attribute that a person finds difficult to negate from himself or to admit lacking. Thus, the meaning boils down to something like: "I seek refuge and protection in the All-Beneficent from you if you are God-conscious, and it is mandatory that you be God-conscious; so let your God-consciousness deter you from accosting me or intending me harm."

Thus, the verse is similar to addressing the believers with such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ** — **and be wary of Allah, should you be believers** (5:57), and His saying: **وَعَلَى اللهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ** — **and put your trust in Allah, should you be believers** (5:23).

It is also possible that in His saying "if you are" (إِنْ كُنْتَ), the "if" (*in*) is negatory, meaning: "You are not God-conscious, since you have violated my privacy and entered without my permission." The first of the two views is more in accord with the context. The claim that "al-Taqi" (التقي) was the name of a wicked or righteous man is not to be taken seriously.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ إِنَّما أَنَا رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلاماً زَكِيًّا** — **He said, ‘I am only a messenger of your Lord that I may give you a pure son’** (19:19). This is the Spirit's reply to Maryam. He began the speech with a restriction (القصر) to indicate that he was not a human as she had thought, thereby removing her fear, and then he gladdened her soul with the good news. The **"pure" (الزكي)** one is he who grows in a wholesome manner and flourishes beautifully.

Among the subtle correspondences in these stories mentioned in the Surah is that He, the Exalted, mentioned Zechariah and that He gave him Yahya; He mentioned Maryam and that He gave her ‘Isa; He mentioned Ibrahim and that He gave him Ishaq and Ya‘qub; and He mentioned Musa and that He gave him Harun (peace be upon them).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَتْ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي غُلامٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِي بَشَرٌ وَلَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا** — **She said, ‘How shall I have a son seeing that no human has ever touched me, nor have I been unchaste?’** (19:20). The "touching" (مس) of a human, by virtue of its contrast with "unchastity" (البغي) — which is adultery — is a metaphor for marriage, though it is more general in itself. Therefore, in the story in Surah Al ‘Imran, it was sufficient to say: **وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِي بَشَرٌ** — **seeing that no human has ever touched me** (3:47). The question is for wonderment, meaning: How shall I have a child when no man has consorted with me before this time, neither through the lawful way of marriage nor through the unlawful way of adultery?

The context testifies that she understood from his saying "that I may give you a son" etc., that he would give him to her immediately. Therefore, she said: "no human has ever touched me, nor have I been unchaste," negating both marriage and adultery in the past.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ كَذلِكِ قالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ** — **He said, ‘So it is. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is simple for Me’’...** (19:21). That is, the Spirit said: The matter is as such, i.e., as I have described it to you. Then he said: "Your Lord says, ‘It is simple for Me’." The clarification of these two sentences has already passed in the story of Zechariah and Yahya (peace be upon them).

His saying: **وَلِنَجْعَلَهُ آيَةً لِلنَّاسِ وَرَحْمَةً مِنَّا** — **‘...and so that We may make him a sign for mankind and a mercy from Us’** (19:21). He mentioned some of the purposes for creating the Messiah in this miraculous manner. It is coordinated with a suppressed clause, meaning: We created him by the breathing of the Spirit without a father for such-and-such reasons, and so that We may make him a sign (آية) for mankind by his creation, and a mercy (رحمة) from Us through his message and the signs performed at his hand. The omission of some purposes and the coordination of others mentioned is frequent in the Quran, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** — **and so that he may be of those who have certainty** (6:75). In this literary technique, there is an intimation that divine purposes are too great for any understanding to encompass or any wording to fully express.

His saying: **وَكانَ أَمْراً مَقْضِيًّا** — **‘...and it is a matter [already] decided’** (19:21). This is an indication of the finality of the decree (القضاء) regarding the matter of this pure boy, such that it cannot be averted by refusal or prayer.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَحَمَلَتْهُ فَانْتَبَذَتْ بِهِ مَكاناً قَصِيًّا** — **So she conceived him, and withdrew with him to a distant place** (19:22). The **"distant" (القصي)** means far away; that is, she became pregnant with the child, then secluded herself and withdrew with him to a place far from her family.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَجاءَهَا الْمَخاضُ إِلى جِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ** — **The birth pangs brought her to the trunk of a palm tree** (19:23) until the end of the verse. **"Bringing" (الإجاءة)** is the causative form of "coming" (جاء); it is said "he brought him" (أجاءه) and "he came with him" (جاء به) with the same meaning. In the verse, it is a metaphor for driving and compelling. **"Birth pangs" (المخاض والطلق)** is the pain of childbirth. The **"trunk of the palm tree" (جذع النخلة)** is its stem. **"Forgotten" (النسي)** — with a *fatha* or *kasra* on the *nun* — is like something insignificant that is normally forgotten. The meaning is: When she secluded herself from her people in a place far from them, the birth pangs drove and compelled her to the trunk of a palm tree that was there to deliver her child. The expression "trunk of the palm tree" rather than "the palm tree" suggests that it was dry and not green. She said, out of shame before the people: "Would that I had died before this and been a forgotten thing (نسيا), something insignificant that is not cared for and is forgotten, not mentioned, so that people would not fall into [slandering] me as they will."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَناداها مِنْ تَحْتِها أَلَّا تَحْزَنِي** — **Thereupon he called her from below her, ‘Do not grieve!’** (19:24) until the end of the two verses. The apparent context is that the subject of "he called her" (فَناداها) is ‘Isa (peace be upon him), not the previously mentioned Spirit. This is supported by its qualification with "from below her" (مِنْ تَحْتِها), for this qualification is more appropriate for the state of the newborn with his mother at the time of birth than for the state of the calling angel with the one he calls. It is also supported by its being surrounded by pronouns referring to ‘Isa (peace be upon him).

It has been said: The pronoun refers to the Spirit, and the fact that the Spirit was "below her" was explained by her being on a hill at the time of birth while the Spirit was standing below the hill and called her from below her. However, there is no evidence for any of this from the wording.

It is not improbable to infer from the sequence of His saying "he called her" (فَناداها) following her saying "Would that I..." etc., that she only said those words at the time of birth or after it, so he (peace be upon him) followed it by saying: "Do not grieve," etc.

His saying: **أَلَّا تَحْزَنِي** — **‘Do not grieve!’** is a consolation for her for the grief and intense distress that had afflicted her. For there is no calamity more bitter or difficult for a pious, ascetic woman — especially if she is a virgin — than to be accused in her honor, especially if she is from a house known for chastity and integrity in its present and past, and especially if it is an accusation against which she has no way to defend herself and the opponent has the argument against her. Therefore, he indicated that she should not speak to anyone, and he took it upon himself to defend her, and that is an argument that no one can refute.

His saying: **قَدْ جَعَلَ رَبُّكِ تَحْتَكِ سَرِيًّا** — **‘Your Lord has made a rivulet [to flow] below you’** (19:24). The **"rivulet" (السري)** is a stream of water. *Sari* (السري) also means noble and exalted. The first meaning is more appropriate to the context, and a clue for it is His subsequent saying: "So eat and drink," as is not hidden.

It has been said: What is meant is the second meaning, and its referent is ‘Isa (peace be upon him). You have already seen that the context does not support this. In any case, the sentence until the end of his speech is a gladdening of Maryam's (peace be upon her) soul.

His saying: **وَهُزِّي إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ تُساقِطْ عَلَيْكِ رُطَباً جَنِيًّا** — **‘Shake the trunk of the palm tree, and it will drop upon you fresh ripe dates’** (19:25). **"Shaking" (الهز)** is vigorous movement. It is reported from al-Farra’ that the Arabs say: "he shook it" (هزه) and "he shook with it" (هز به). **"Dropping" (المساقطة)** is the act of causing to fall. The pronoun in "it will drop" (تُساقِطْ) refers to the palm tree. Attributing the shaking to the trunk and the dropping to the palm tree is not devoid of an intimation that the palm tree was dry and only became green, leafed, and bore fresh ripe dates at that very moment. **"Ripe dates" (الرطب)** are ripened dates. **"Freshly picked" (الجني)** means that which is harvested. It is mentioned in *al-Qamus* — as reported — that *jani* is only said of that which is picked at that very moment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكُلِي وَاشْرَبِي وَقَرِّي عَيْناً** — **‘So eat and drink and be consoled’** (19:26). The "settling of the eye" (قرار العين) is a metaphor for joy. It is said: "May Allah settle your eye," meaning: May He gladden you. The meaning is: Eat of the fresh ripe dates that fall and drink from the rivulet that is below you and be in a state of joy without grieving. Partaking in eating and drinking is a sign of joy and delight, for one afflicted by calamity is too preoccupied to enjoy delicious food and pleasant drink; his calamity occupies him. The meaning is: Eat of the fresh ripe dates and drink from the rivulet and be in joy — because of what Allah has bestowed upon you — without grieving. As for what you fear of the people's accusation and questioning, then maintain silence and do not speak to anyone, for I shall suffice you against them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِمَّا تَرَيِنَّ مِنَ الْبَشَرِ أَحَداً فَقُولِي إِنِّي نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمنِ صَوْماً فَلَنْ أُكَلِّمَ الْيَوْمَ إِنْسِيًّا** — **‘And if you see any human, say, ‘‘Indeed I have vowed a fast to the All-beneficent, so I will not speak to any human today’’ ’** (19:26). What is meant by "fast" (صوما) is a fast of silence (صوم الصمت), as indicated by the consequence in His saying: "so I will not speak to any human today." Likewise, it is inferred from the context that it was a prescribed practice at that time; therefore, it was presented as a valid excuse. **"Human" (الإنسي)** is attributed to *ins* (humankind) as opposed to the Jinn, and it refers to an individual human being.

His saying: "And if you see" (فَإِمَّا تَرَيِنَّ) etc., the *ma* is extra, and the original is: "If you see a human, then say" etc. The meaning is: If you see a human and he speaks to you or asks you about the matter of the child, then say etc. What is meant by "saying" (القول) is conveying the meaning through gesture, for sometimes conveying through gesture is called "saying." It is reported from al-Farra’ that the Arabs call everything that reaches a person "speech" (كلام), by whatever means it reaches, as long as it is not emphasized with the verbal noun (المصدر); if it is emphasized, it can only be literal speech.

It is not improbable to infer from His saying: "then say, ‘Indeed I have vowed a fast to the All-Beneficent’," with the help of the context, that He commanded her to intend the fast at that moment and vow it to Allah for herself, so it would not be an announcement of something that has no reality.

His saying: "And if you see" etc., is in any case a consequence of His saying: "and be consoled." The meaning is: Do not speak to any human and do not answer anyone who asks you about my matter; rather, refer the matter to me, for I shall suffice you in answering their questions and I shall defend against their contention.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَتَتْ بِهِ قَوْمَها تَحْمِلُهُ قالُوا يا مَرْيَمُ لَقَدْ جِئْتِ شَيْئاً فَرِيًّا** — **Then she brought him to her people, carrying him. They said, ‘O Mary, you have certainly come up with an amazing thing!’** (19:27). The two pronouns in "with him" (بِهِ) and "carrying him" (تَحْمِلُهُ) refer to ‘Isa. The question is one of disapproval (إنكاري) prompted by what they witnessed of her strange situation, despite her history of asceticism and seclusion. She was the daughter of ‘Imran and from the family of the saintly Harun. **"Amazing" (الفري)** means something great and unprecedented. It has been said: It is from *iftira’* (افتراء) meaning a lie, as a metaphor for something foul and reprehensible. The following verse supports the first meaning. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أُخْتَ هارُونَ ما كانَ أَبُوكِ امْرَأَ سَوْءٍ وَما كانَتْ أُمُّكِ بَغِيًّا** — **‘O sister of Aaron! Your father was not an evil man, nor was your mother unchaste’** (19:28). It is mentioned in *al-Majma‘* that regarding the identity of Harun, there are four views: First, that he was a righteous man from the Israelites to whom every righteous person was compared; thus, "sisterhood" here means resemblance, and the meaning of "O sister of Aaron" is "O you who resemble Aaron." Second, that he was her brother from her father, not her mother. Third, that what is meant is Aaron, the brother of Moses the Interlocutor (الكليم); thus, "sisterhood" means lineage, as one says "brother of Tamim." Fourth, that he was a man known for immorality and corruption. [End of summary]. **"Unchaste" (البغي)** means an adulteress. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَشارَتْ إِلَيْهِ قالُوا كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَنْ كانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا** — **Thereat she pointed to him. They said, ‘How can we speak to one who is yet a baby in the cradle?’** (19:29). Her pointing to him was a referral of them to him so that he might answer them and reveal to them the truth of the matter. This was done by her in accordance with what he had commanded her when he was born, by his saying: "And if you see any human, say, ‘Indeed I have vowed a fast to the All-beneficent, so I will not speak to any human today’," as discussed previously.

The **"cradle" (المهد)** is the bed prepared for a child in which he is placed and put to sleep. It has been said: What is meant by "cradle" in the verse is his mother's lap. It has also been said: The nursery or the rocker. It has also been said: The place where he was settled. All this is because she had not yet prepared a cradle for him. The truth is that the verse is apparent in its meaning, and there is no evidence that she had not prepared a cradle for him at that time. Perhaps the people rushed to her and spoke to her after she had returned to her house, settled in it, and prepared a cradle or a rocker for him, which is also called a cradle.

The verse has been challenged on the grounds that the use of the word "was" (كان) disrupts the meaning, for what the context requires is their astonishment at speaking to one who *is* a baby in the cradle, not one who *was* a baby in the cradle before that. For everyone whom people speak to, whether man or woman, was a baby in the cradle some time before the speaking, and there is no astonishment in that.

It was answered, first: that the past tense (الزمان الماضي) can be distant or near, adjacent to the present. The meaning would only be corrupted if the referent of "was" (كان) in the verse were the distant past. As for it being the near past connected to the present — which is the time of the speaking — there is no danger of corruption. This view is by al-Zamakhshari in *al-Kashshaf*.

However, while this repels the objection, it does not align with the nature of their denial. For they were only denying speaking to him and his speaking because he was *actually* a baby in the cradle, not because he was a baby in the cradle a short time before. Thus, "was" (كان) would be redundant (زائدة).

It was answered, second: that His saying "How can we speak" (كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ) is to recount a past state, and "who" (مَنْ) is a relative pronoun. The meaning is: "How can we speak to those who are described as being in the cradle? — meaning: we have not spoken to them until now, so how should we speak to this one?" This view is also by al-Zamakhshari in *al-Kashshaf*.

However, although more than one scholar has found it good, it is a meaning far from [natural] understanding!

It was answered, third: that "was" (كان) is redundant for emphasis without indicating time, and "who was in the cradle" (مَنْ كانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ) is a subject and predicate, and "a baby" (صبيا) is an emphatic circumstantial qualifier (حال مؤكدة).

However, there is no evidence for this. Furthermore, it is a redundancy that causes ambiguity without necessity. Moreover, it has been said that the redundant "was" (كان) indicates time even if it does not indicate an event.

It was answered, fourth: that "who" (مَنْ) in the verse is conditional, and "was in the cradle a baby" (كانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا) is its condition, and His saying "How can we speak" (كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ) is in the place of the result. The meaning is: "Whoever is a baby in the cradle, it is impossible to speak to him." The past tense in a conditional sentence carries the meaning of the future, so there is no problem.

However, this is an obvious affectation (تكلف).

It can be said: "was" (كان) is detached from indicating time because of the meaning of condition and result in the speech. For it is in the sense of: "Whoever is a baby, it is impossible to speak to him." Or, "was" (كان) was brought to indicate the establishment of the attribute for its subject — an establishment that dictates its past occurrence, its realization in it, and its necessity for it — such as His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً** — **Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human messenger?’** (17:93), meaning: humanity and messengership are realized in me, so I cannot do what a human messenger cannot do. And His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ قُتِلَ مَظْلُوماً فَقَدْ جَعَلْنا لِوَلِيِّهِ سُلْطاناً فَلا يُسْرِفْ فِي الْقَتْلِ إِنَّهُ كانَ مَنْصُوراً** — **Whoever is killed wrongfully, We have certainly given his heir an authority. But let him not commit any excess in killing. Indeed he is helped [by the Law]** (17:33), meaning: help is necessary for him by Our decree, like the necessity of a past attribute for its subject. The meaning would be: "How can we speak to a baby in the cradle, deeply immersed in his infancy, whose state is that he has remained and will remain in his infancy for a period of time?" And Allah knows best.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللهِ آتانِيَ الْكِتابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا** — **He said, ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet’** (19:30). He (peace be upon him) began the reply without addressing the problem of the birth with which they were attacking Maryam (peace be upon her). This is because his speaking in his infancy is a miraculous sign (آية معجزة), and the truth he announced leaves no doubt for any doubter regarding his matter. Furthermore, he greeted himself at the end of his speech, thereby testifying to his own integrity and safety from every impurity and vileness, and from his integrity is the purity of his birth.

He began by saying: "Indeed I am a servant of Allah" (إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللهِ), confessing his servitude (العبودية) to Allah to nullify the extremism of the extremists and to establish the argument against them, just as he concluded with the like of that when he said: "Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him."

In His saying: "He has given me the Book" (آتانِيَ الْكِتابَ), there is an announcement of the giving of the Book, and it is apparently the Gospel. In His saying: "and made me a prophet" (وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا), there is a notification of his prophethood. The difference between prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة) has already passed in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume of the book. He was a prophet at that time, then Allah chose him for messengership. The apparent meaning of the speech is that he had already been given the Book and prophethood, not that this was an announcement of what would happen in the future.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَعَلَنِي مُبارَكاً أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُ وَأَوْصانِي بِالصَّلاةِ وَالزَّكاةِ ما دُمْتُ حَيًّا** — **‘He has made me blessed, wherever I may be, and He has enjoined me to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat as long as I live’** (19:31). His (peace be upon him) being "blessed" (مباركاً) wherever he might be means his being a locus for every blessing (البركة) — and blessing is the growth of good. He was beneficial to people, teaching them beneficial knowledge, calling them to righteous deeds, raising them with a pure upbringing, healing the blind and the leper, rectifying the strong, and helping the weak.

His saying: "and He has enjoined me to the prayer and the zakat" etc., is an indication of the legislation of prayer (الصلاة) and zakat (الزكاة) in his law. **"Prayer" (الصلاة)** is the specific devotional turning toward Allah, the Glorified, and **"zakat" (الزكاة)** is financial spending. This is what has been established in the usage of the Quran whenever it mentions prayer and zakat and pairs them together, which occurs in over twenty places. Thus, the claim of those who say that zakat here means the purification of the soul (تزكية النفس) rather than financial spending is not to be considered.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَبَرًّا بِوالِدَتِي وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّاراً شَقِيًّا** — **‘...and to be good to my mother, and He has not made me a tyrannical wretch’** (19:32). That is: He made me compassionate and kind to people, and part of that is that I am dutiful to my mother and I am not a tyrannical wretch toward other people. The **"tyrant" (الجبار)** is he who compels people and is not compelled by them. It is reported from Ibn ‘Ata’ that the tyrant is he who does not give counsel, and the wretch (الشقي) is he who does not accept counsel.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالسَّلامُ عَلَيَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدْتُ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا** — **‘Peace is to me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive’** (19:33). This is a salutation from him upon himself in the three universal stations that await him in his existence. Its clarification has already passed at the end of the preceding story of Yahya.

Yes, there is a difference between the two salutations: the "peace" (السلام) in the story of Yahya is indefinite (نكرة), indicating the type, while in this story it is prefixed with the definite article of genus (لام الجنس), signifying through its absolute nature an all-encompassing scope. Another difference is that the one who saluted Yahya was Allah, the Glorified, while the one who saluted ‘Isa was himself.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذلِكَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ قَوْلَ الْحَقِّ الَّذِي فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ** — **That is Jesus, son of Mary, a statement of the truth concerning which they are in doubt** (19:34). It is apparent that this verse and the one following it are parenthetical, and the third verse: "Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord," is a continuation of the speech of ‘Isa (peace be upon him).

His saying: "That is Jesus, son of Mary" (ذلِكَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ) — the demonstration (الإشارة) refers to the sum of what has been narrated of his matter and explained of his description. That is: the one whose manner of birth We mentioned and whose description he himself gave to the people — of his servitude, his being given the Book, and his being made a prophet — is Jesus, son of Mary.

His saying: "a statement of the truth" (قَوْلَ الْحَقِّ) is in the accusative case due to a suppressed verb, meaning: "I say the statement of the truth." His saying: "concerning which they are in doubt" (الَّذِي فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ) — meaning they doubt or dispute — is a description of ‘Isa. The meaning is: That is Jesus, son of Mary, concerning whom they doubt or dispute.

It has been said: What is meant by "statement of the truth" is the "Word of Truth" (كلمة الحق), which is ‘Isa (peace be upon him), because Allah, the Glorified, named him His Word in His saying: **وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى مَرْيَمَ** — **and His Word that He cast toward Mary** (4:171), and His saying: **يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنْهُ** — **gives you the good news of a Word from Him** (3:45), and His saying: **بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ** — **of a Word from Allah** (3:39). Accordingly, "statement of the truth" is in the accusative for praise (المدح). The first meaning is supported by His saying, the Exalted, regarding this meaning at the end of the story in Surah Al ‘Imran: **الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ** — **This is the truth from your Lord, so do not be among the doubters** (3:60).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما كانَ لِلَّهِ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ مِنْ وَلَدٍ سُبْحانَهُ إِذا قَضى أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** — **It is not for Allah to take a son. Immaculate is He! When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is** (19:35). This is a negation and refutation of what the Christians said regarding the sonship of the Messiah. His saying: "When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’" is an argument established for that. He expressed it with the term "decree" (القضاء) to indicate the criterion of impossibility.

That is because a son is only desired for assistance in needs, and Allah, the Glorified, is free of need for that; no intended matter fails His will. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, "Be!" and it is.

Furthermore, a son consists of parts of the father's existence that he separates and then nurtures gradually until he becomes an individual like him. Allah, the Glorified, is free of need for resorting to gradualness (التدريج) in His act, and He has no equal. Rather, what He wills comes to be as He willed it without delay or gradualness, and without it being like Him. A similar meaning has already passed in the commentary on His saying: **وَقالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللهُ وَلَداً سُبْحانَهُ** — **And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!** (2:116) in the first volume of the book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ اللهَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ** — **‘Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path’** (19:36). This is coordinated with his saying: "Indeed I am a servant of Allah," and it is from the speech of ‘Isa (peace be upon him). Evidence for this is the occurrence of the exact same verse in the recounted version of his call to his people in his story in Surah Al ‘Imran. A similar instance is in Surah al-Zukhruf where he said: **إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ فَاخْتَلَفَ الْأَحْزابُ مِنْ بَيْنِهِمْ فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْ عَذابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ** — **‘Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path.’ But the factions differed among themselves; so woe to the wrongdoers from the punishment of a painful day** (43:64-65).

Thus, there is no basis for the possibility suggested by some that the verse is a resumption and a beginning of speech from Allah, the Glorified, or a command from Him to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to say: "Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord" etc. Furthermore, the context of the verses does not support either of the two views. It is from the speech of ‘Isa (peace be upon him); he concluded his speech with the confession of Lordship (المربوبية) just as he began his speech with the testimony of servitude, in order to cut off the root of the extremists' excess regarding him and to complete the argument against them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاخْتَلَفَ الْأَحْزابُ مِنْ بَيْنِهِمْ فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ مَشْهَدِ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ** — **But the factions differed among themselves; so woe to the faithless for the scene of a terrible day** (19:37). **"Factions" (الأحزاب)** is the plural of *hizb* (حزب), which is a group separated in its opinion from others. Thus, the "differing of the factions" is the saying of each of them regarding him (peace be upon him) contrary to what the others say. He only said "among themselves" (مِنْ بَيْنِهِمْ) because among them were those who remained firm on the truth. It has been suggested that "from" (مِنْ) is extra and the original is "the factions differed between them," but this is as you see [weak].

"Woe" (الويل) is a word of threat signifying the intensification of punishment. **"Scene" (المشهد)** is a mim-infinitive meaning "witnessing" (الشهود).

The detailed discussion of the stories of the Messiah (peace be upon him) and the general differences of the Christians regarding him has already passed in the third volume of the book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَسْمِعْ بِهِمْ وَأَبْصِرْ يَوْمَ يَأْتُونَنا لكِنِ الظَّالِمُونَ الْيَوْمَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ** — **How well they will hear and how well they will see on the day they come to Us! But today the wrongdoers are in manifest error** (19:38). That is: How well they will hear and see the truth on the day they come to Us and return to Us — which is the Day of Resurrection — so the face of truth will become clear to them regarding that which they differed in, just as He recounted their confession of it in His saying: **رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ** — **‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard. So send us back [to the world] that we may act righteously. Indeed [now] we have certainty’** (32:12).

As for the exception (الاستدراك) in His saying: "But today the wrongdoers are in manifest error," it is to repel the illusion that if they hear and see on the Day of Resurrection and the truth is revealed to them, they will be guided and thus become happy through the attainment of knowledge and certainty. So He clarified that they do not benefit from that nor are they guided; rather, the wrongdoers today are in manifest error because of their wrongdoing.

This is because that day is a day of recompense, not a day of action. Thus, they will only face today what they sent forth of deeds and their effects, and what they earned in their yesterday for their today. As for starting a deed on the Day of Resurrection for which they expect recompense tomorrow, today has no tomorrow. In other words, these people have had the disposition of error (ملكة الضلال) firmly rooted in them in the world, and they have been cut off from the realm of choice (موطن الاختيار) by the arrival of death. Thus, they have no choice but to live by necessity upon the error they prepared for themselves, with no turning away from it. Therefore, the revelation of the truth and the manifestation of reality do not benefit them.

Some have mentioned that what is meant by the verse is a command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to make the people hear and see by explaining that on the day they are present for reckoning and recompense, they will be in manifest error. This is a trivial view that does not fit the verse at all.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْحَسْرَةِ إِذْ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ وَهُمْ فِي غَفْلَةٍ وَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — **Warn them of the Day of Regret, when the matter will be decided, while they are [now] in heedlessness and do not have faith** (19:39). The apparent context is that His saying "when the matter will be decided" (إِذْ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ) is an explanation for His saying "the Day of Regret" (يَوْمَ الْحَسْرَةِ). In it is an indication that regret only comes to them from the direction of the deciding of the matter. A decision only necessitates regret if it is such that through it, the one decided against loses that which is the delight of his eye, the wish of his soul, and the core of his happiness — which he used to estimate attaining for himself and without which he saw no goodness in life — because of his heart's attachment to it and his infatuation with it. It is known that a human does not accept the loss of what is of this nature, even if he were to endure any hardship to preserve it, unless heedlessness (الغفلة) diverts him from it, so he falls short in its regard. Therefore, He followed the speech with His saying: "while they are in heedlessness and do not have faith."

The meaning — and Allah knows best — is: Warn them of a day in which the matter will be decided, so permanent destruction will become inevitable for them, and they will be cut off from their eternal happiness in which is the delight of their eyes. They will then regret it with a regret that cannot be measured, because they were heedless in the world and did not follow the path that would guide them and lead them to it through steadfastness, which is faith in Allah alone and His transcendence above having a son or a partner.

What we have presented is sufficient to cover the various views they have brought in the commentary of the verse. And Allah is the Guide.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ الْأَرْضَ وَمَنْ عَلَيْها وَإِلَيْنا يُرْجَعُونَ** — **Indeed it is We who shall inherit the earth and whoever is on it, and to Us they shall be brought back** (19:40). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: **"Inheritance" (الوراثة والإرث)** is the transfer of a possession to you from another without a contract or what functions as a contract. That which is transferred from the deceased is named thus... It is said: "I inherited wealth from Zayd" and "I inherited Zayd." [End of quote].

The verse — as it were — is a confirmation and a type of approximation for His saying in the previous verse: "the matter will be decided." The meaning is: This decision is easy and simple for Us, for We inherit the earth and them, and to Us they return. The inheritance of the earth is that they leave it through death, so it remains for Allah, the Exalted. The inheritance of whoever is on it is that they die, so what was in their hands remains for Allah, the Glorified. Accordingly, the two sentences "We inherit the earth" and "and whoever is on it" are in the sense of a single sentence: "We inherit the earth from them."

It is possible to interpret the verse with a more subtle meaning than that, which is to mean that Allah, the Glorified, is the One who remains after the annihilation of everything. Thus, He is the One who remains after the annihilation of the earth, possessing from it what it used to possess of existence and the effects of existence. He is the One who remains after the annihilation of man, possessing what he used to possess, just as He restricted kingship to Himself in His saying: **لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** — **‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’** (40:16), and His saying: **وَنَرِثُهُ ما يَقُولُ وَيَأْتِينا فَرْداً** — **We shall inherit from him what he says, and he will come to Us alone** (19:80).

The meaning of this inheritance returns to the return of all and their gathering to Him, the Exalted. Thus, His saying: "and to Us they shall be brought back" would be an explanatory coordination (عطف تفسير) and in the position of a rationale for the second sentence or for the sum of the two sentences, by giving preponderance to those with intellect over others, or because everything will emerge on that day alive and sentient.

This view is safer from the suspicion of repetition (التكرار) that is necessary for the first view, for the speech according to it would be like saying: "I inherited Zayd's wealth and Zayd."

The conclusion of the speech on the story of ‘Isa (peace be upon him) with this verse is not devoid of appropriateness. For His inheritance, the Exalted, is among the arguments for the negation of a son. For a son is only desired to be an heir to his father; thus, He who inherits everything is in no need of a son.

## (A Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma‘*: It is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "That he — meaning Gabriel — took the opening of her garment and breathed into it a breath — so the child became complete in the womb at that very moment — just as a child becomes complete in the wombs of women in nine months — so she came out of the bathhouse while she was pregnant, heavy with child — her maternal aunt looked at her and disapproved of her state — so Maryam went on her way, ashamed before her aunt and before Zechariah." It has been said: The duration of her pregnancy was nine hours; this is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

**I say:** In some narrations (الروايات), it is mentioned that the duration of her pregnancy was six months.

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَتْ يَا لَيْتَنِي مِتُّ قَبْلَ هَٰذَا** "She said, ‘I wish I had died before this’" [19:23]—the author notes that she only wished for death—until he says—and it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "Because she did not see among her people anyone of right guidance (رشيد) or keen discernment (ذا فراسة) who would exonerate her from evil."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قَدْ جَعَلَ رَبُّكِ تَحْتَكِ سَرِيًّا** "your Lord has furnished a rivulet below you" [19:24]—it is said: Jibril struck with his foot and sweet water appeared—and it is also said: rather, ‘Isa struck with his foot and a flowing spring of water appeared; this is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Tabarani in *al-Saghir* and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from al-Bara’ ibn ‘Azib from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: **قَدْ جَعَلَ رَبُّكِ تَحْتَكِ سَرِيًّا**—he said: "A river (النهر)."

**I say:** In another narration therein from Ibn ‘Umar from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "It is a river that Allah brought forth for her so that she might drink from it."

In *al-Khisal*, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام) in the "Hadith of the Four Hundred": "There is nothing a pregnant woman eats or treats herself with that is better than fresh dates (الرطب)—Allah, the Exalted, said to Maryam: **وَهُزِّي إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ تُسَاقِطْ عَلَيْكِ رُطَبًا جَنِيًّا فَكُلِي وَاشْرَبِي وَقَرِّي عَيْنًا** ‘Shake the stem of the palm tree towards yourself; it will drop fresh ripe dates upon you. So eat and drink and be consoled.’" [19:25-26]

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in several narrations through Sunni chains from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and through Shi‘i chains from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Jarrah al-Mada’ini from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Fasting (الصيام) is not from food and drink alone." Then he said: "Maryam said: **إِنِّي نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ صَوْمًا** ‘I have vowed a fast to the All-Beneficent’ [19:26]—meaning a fast of silence (صمتا)—and in another version: meaning silence—so when you fast, guard your tongues, lower your gazes, and do not dispute or envy one another." (The hadith continues).

In the book *Sa‘d al-Su‘ud* by Ibn Tawus, from the book of ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn Muhammad al-Azdi: Simak ibn Harb narrated to me from al-Mughirah ibn Shu‘bah: That the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent him to Najran, and they said: "Do you not read: **يَا أُخْتَ هَارُونَ** ‘O sister of Aaron’ [19:28], while between them [‘Isa and Harun] there is such-and-such [a long period of time]?" He mentioned that to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Why did you not tell them that they used to name [their children] after their prophets and the righteous (الصالحين) among them?"

**I say:** This hadith is cited in *al-Durr al-Manthur* in detail, and in *Majma‘ al-Bayan* in brief, from al-Mughirah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The meaning of the hadith is that the intended Harun in His saying: **يَا أُخْتَ هَارُونَ** "O sister of Aaron" was a man named after the Prophet Harun, the brother of Musa (عليه السلام); there is no indication in it that he was among the righteous, as some have imagined.

In *al-Kafi* and *Ma‘ani al-Akhbar*, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلَنِي مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنْتُ** "and He has made me blessed, wherever I may be" [19:31], he said: "Very beneficial (نفاعا)."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from the masters of the books from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the wording of the hadith is: The Prophet said regarding the saying of ‘Isa (عليه السلام): **وَجَعَلَنِي مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنْتُ**—he said: "He made me very beneficial (نفاعا) to people wherever I turn."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn ‘Adi and Ibn ‘Asakir extracted from Ibn Mas‘ud from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding: **وَجَعَلَنِي مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنْتُ**—he said: "A teacher and an educator (مؤدبا)."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Burayd al-Kunasi, who said: I asked Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام): "Was ‘Isa ibn Maryam, when he spoke in the cradle, a Proof (حجة) of Allah over the people of his time?" He replied: "He was on that day a Prophet (نبيا) and a Proof for Allah, though not yet a Messenger (مرسل). Have you not heard his saying when he said: **إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ آتَانِيَ الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا وَجَعَلَنِي مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنْتُ وَأَوْصَانِي بِالصَّلَاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا** ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet. He has made me blessed, wherever I may be, and He has enjoined me to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat as long as I live’ [19:30-31]?"

I said: "So was he on that day a Proof for Allah over Zakariyya in that state—while he was in the cradle?" He replied: "‘Isa was in that state a Sign (آية) from Allah and a Mercy (رحمة) from Allah for Maryam when he spoke and expressed on her behalf. He was a Prophet and a Proof over whoever heard his speech in that state. Then he became silent and did not speak until two years had passed for him. Zakariyya was the Proof for Allah (عز وجل) after the silence of ‘Isa for two years.

Then Zakariyya died, and his son Yahya inherited the Book and wisdom (الحكمة) from him while he was a young boy. Have you not heard the saying of the Almighty (عز وجل): **يَا يَحْيَىٰ خُذِ الْكِتَابَ بِقُوَّةٍ وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا** ‘O Yahya! Hold on to the Book with might.’ And We gave him judgement while he was still a lad’ [19:12]? When he reached seven years of age, he spoke with Prophethood (النبوة) and Messengership (الرسالة) when Allah revealed to him. Thus, ‘Isa was the Proof over Yahya and over all people.

The earth, O Abu Khalid, does not remain for a single day without a Proof (حجة) from Allah over the people, since the day Allah created Adam (عليه السلام) and settled him on the earth." (The hadith continues).

In the same source, with its chain to Safwan ibn Yahya, who said: I said to Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام): "We used to ask you before Allah granted you Abu Ja‘far [Imam al-Jawad], and you used to say: ‘Allah will grant me a boy.’ Now Allah has granted him to you, so our eyes are comforted. May Allah never show us your [final] day—but if something were to happen, to whom [should we turn]?" He pointed with his hand to Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) while he was standing before him. I said: "May I be your ransom! This is a child of three years?" He said: "That does not harm him at all; ‘Isa stood as the Proof (الحجة) when he was [only] three years old."

**I say:** What is mentioned in some other narrations is close to this.

In the same source, with its chain to Mu‘awiyah ibn Wahb, who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the best thing by which servants draw near to their Lord, and what is most beloved of that to Allah (عز وجل)? He said: "I know of nothing after Gnosis (المعرفة) better than this prayer (الصلاة). Do you not see that the righteous servant ‘Isa ibn Maryam said: **وَأَوْصَانِي بِالصَّلَاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا** ‘and He has enjoined me to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat as long as I live’ [19:31]?"

In *‘Uyun al-Akhbar*, with its chain to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a hadith: "And among them [the major sins] is the mistreatment of parents (عقوق الوالدين), because Allah (عز وجل) has made the one who is undutiful (العاق) a wretched tyrant (جبارا شقيا) in His saying, narrating from ‘Isa (عليه السلام): **وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَتِي وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا** ‘and [He has made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a wretched tyrant’ [19:32]."

**I say:** The apparent meaning of the narration is that he (عليه السلام) took his saying: **وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا** "and He has not made me a wretched tyrant" as an appositional explanation (عطف تفسير) for his saying: **وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَتِي** "and [He has made me] dutiful to my mother."

In *al-Majma‘*, Muslim narrated in the *Sahih* with the chain to Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When the people of Paradise enter Paradise and the people of the Fire enter the Fire, it will be said: ‘O people of Paradise!’ and they will look up and see. And it will be said: ‘O people of the Fire!’ and they will look up and see. Then Death (الموت) will be brought as if it were a salt-and-pepper ram (كبش أملح). It will be said to them: ‘Do you recognize Death?’ They will say: ‘This is it, this is it,’ and everyone will have recognized it." He said:

"Then it will be brought forward and slaughtered. Then it will be said: ‘O people of Paradise, eternity (خلود) and no more death! O people of the Fire, eternity and no more death!’" He said: "That is His saying: **وَأَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْحَسْرَةِ** ‘Warn them of the Day of Regret’ [19:39]."

He said: Our companions [the Shi‘i scholars] narrated it from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), then it added at the end: "The people of Paradise will rejoice with such joy that if anyone were to die [of joy] that day, they would die of joy. And the people of the Fire will groan with such a groan that if anyone were to die, they would die."

**I say:** This meaning was narrated by others besides Muslim among the masters of the collections, such as al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, al-Tabari, and others, from Abu Sa‘id, Abu Hurayrah, Ibn Mas‘ud, and Ibn ‘Abbas.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ الْأَرْضَ وَمَنْ عَلَيْهَا** "Indeed We shall inherit the earth and whoever is on it" [19:40], he said: "Everything that Allah created, Allah shall inherit on the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** This is the second of the two meanings of the verse mentioned previously in its commentary.
